segs.utf8:
Hello and welcome to Real Easy English 
from BBC Learning English. I'm Neil.

And I'm Georgie. In this podcast 
we have real conversations in
easy English about a different topic each week.
And you can read along with the podcast using the script on our website:
BBC Learning English dot com.
Hi, Neil. How are things?

Very good, thank you.
How are you, Georgie?
Yes, I'm pretty good, thank you, as well.
So, Neil, what are we talking about today?

We are talking about animals.
We'll be talking about pets.
OK, Neil. So do you have any pets?

No, I don't have any pets,
but I know you do, Georgie.
Yes, I do. I have a black dog called Rafi.

— Rafi?
— Yeah.
Did you choose the name?

Partly, but it was a decision
we made as a family.
Mm. Did you have pets as a child?
Yes, I did.

We used to have cats, actually,
we started with cats

and then we got a dog
and then we also had hamsters.
What about you, Neil? Did you have pets?
No, we didn't have pets when I was young.
Oh, I did have a goldfish called Gilbert.

But then, later on, when I was a teenager,
my parents got a cat and a dog.

— Wow.
— Mm.

Did you have a good relationship
with Gilbert?
I don't remember Gilbert, sadly.
But we had a cat that I liked very much

and I've never really liked dogs,
I'm afraid.
So, Neil, you don't have any pets now

and you said you don't like dogs,
but will you ever get one?
No, Georgie, I don't think so.
I will never get a dog.

That's OK. I mean, they're not for
everyone. What was your cat like?

The cat made me feel very calm,
because she sat on my lap and purred.

You just used the word purr.
Can you explain what that is?

Yeah.
'Purring' is 'the noise that cats make'.
Not miaow. Miaow is...
Purring is...

And the cat made that noise
and made me feel calm.

Neil, that was a really good purr sound.
I would say it was purr-fect!
So, Georgie, what was your first dog like?
My first dog was really lovely.

I actually used to say
that she was my best friend.
And we actually have a common expression to describe dogs,
which is man's best friend.

'Man' here means 'human',
so human's best friend.

That's because dogs
are usually quite loyal.

OK, so, Georgie, we've talked about dogs
and cats, those kind of pets,
and hamsters and goldfish.

But have you ever thought
about getting an exotic pet like a snake?
Erm, no, not really.

When I think of pets,
I think of fluffy animals with fur.

But I do have a friend that got a scorpion
recently, which I really don't understand.
Would you ever get an exotic pet?

No, absolutely not. I think
I would be scared of an exotic pet,

— to be honest.
— Yes. Yeah.

— They just seem dangerous.
— Mm.

Let's recap the vocabulary
we learned during the conversation.
We had 'ever', which means 'at any time'.

And you asked me Georgie,
will I ever get a dog?

We've had 'never',
which means 'not at any time'.

So, Neil, you said
you will never get a dog.

We heard 'man's best friend', which is
'a common expression to describe dogs'.

And 'purring',
'the noise a cat makes when it's happy'.

That's it for this episode of 
Real Easy English. You can find
See you then, goodbye.
Bye!
